    startDate: string;
    endDate: string;
    Budget: number;
  }
  
  
  export interface ILodgingData {
    type: string;
    name: string;
    price: number;
    rating: number;
    numberOfRatings: number;
    image: string;
    location: {
      city: string;
      state: string;
      country: string;
    };
  }
  
  
  export const fetchRecommendations = async (userInput: IUserInput): Promise<ILodgingData[]> => {
    const { mood, company, startDate, endDate, Budget } = userInput;
  
   
    const queryParams = new URLSearchParams({
      mood,
      company,
      startDate,
      endDate,
      Budget: Budget.toString(),
    });
  
    try {
      
      const response = await fetch(`http://3.109.1.79:8080/api/user/mood/destination?${queryParams}`);
      
      // Check if the response is OK
      if (!response.ok) {
        throw new Error(`API Error: ${response.statusText}`);
      }
  
      
      const data = await response.json();
      return data as ILodgingData[]; 
    } catch (error) {
      console.error('Error fetching recommendations:', error);
      throw error; 
    }
  };

Snehehehehehe: import React, { useState } from 'react';

function LoginForm() {
  const [email, setEmail] = useState('');
  const [phone, setPhone] = useState('');

  const handleLogin = (e) => {
    e.preventDefault();
    console.log('Email:', email);
    console.log('Phone:', phone);
    // Add your login logic here
  };

  return (
    <div className="bg-cover bg-center h-screen" style={{ backgroundImage: "url('https://www.overwaterbungalows.net/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/d06328ca.jpg')" }}>
      <div className="absolute top-10 left-10">
        <p className="text-white italic hover-expand text-2xl font-bold">V.I.B.E.</p>
      </div>
      <div className="flex items-center justify-center h-full">
        <div className="bg-gray-800 bg-opacity-80 p-10 rounded-lg shadow-lg w-full max-w-md custom-backdrop">
          <h2 className="text-3xl font-bold text-center text-white mb-2 italic hover-expand">Log In</h2>
          <p className="text-center text-gray-300 mb-8 hover-expand">Please Enter Your Details</p>
          <form onSubmit={handleLogin}>
            <div className="mb-4">
              <label className="block text-gray-300 mb-2" htmlFor="email">Email</label>
              <div className="relative">
                <input
                  className="w-full p-3 pl-10 bg-gray-700 text-white rounded focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-red-500"
                  id="email"
                  placeholder="Enter Your Email"
                  type="email"
                  value={email}
                  onChange={(e) => setEmail(e.target.value)}
                />
                <i className="fas fa-envelope absolute left-3 top-3 text-gray-400"></i>
              </div>
            </div>
            <div className="mb-4">
              <label className="block text-gray-300 mb-2" htmlFor="phone">Phone Number</label>
              <div className="relative">
                <input
                  className="w-full p-3 pl-10 bg-gray-700 text-white rounded focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-red-500"
                  id="phone"
                  placeholder="Enter Your Phone Number"
                  type="tel"
                  value={phone}
                  onChange={(e) => setPhone(e.target.value)}
                />
                <i className="fas fa-phone absolute left-3 top-3 text-gray-400"></i>
              </div>
            </div>
            <div className="flex justify-between mb-4">
              <button
                type="button"
                className="bg-red-500 text-white py-2 px-4 rounded hover:bg-red-600 hover-expand"
                onClick={() => {
                  setEmail('');
                  setPhone('');
                }}
              >
                Cancel
              </button>
              <button type="submit" className="bg-red-500 text-white py-2 px-4 rounded hover:bg-red-600 hover-expand">
                Log In
              </button>
            </div>
          </form>
          <p className="text-center text-gray-400 mt-4">
            Don't Have An Account?
            <a className="text-red-500 hover:underline" href="#">
              Sign Up
            </a>
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

export default LoginForm;

Parichay: Description: Develop a system that automatically generates videos using frame interpolation techniques given a WMS service that provides satellite imagery at regular intervals (e.g. every 30 minutes) and a bounding box. The AI based video generator should interpolate frames (for e.g. at every minute between 30 minutes) for smooth visualization of moving objects. This video should be displayed on an interactive browser based map (using openlayers or leaflet). Challenge: Frame interpolation of objects such as clouds which are deformable and even appear disappear between frames. Overlaying videos on web based map visualization libraries is straightforward on commercial libraries such as mapbox. Developing the same functionality for modern open-source webgis libraries will be an added effort. Usage: Can be implemented for visualization on VEDAS/MOSDAC/BHUVAN Users: WebGIS application developers and the end users of these applications. Available Solutions (if Yes, reasons for not using them): Individual components are available, comprehensive and proven solution does not exist. Desired Outcome: The tool should be compatible with and OGC compatible WMS service. Should demonstrate video visualization overlay on an OpenLayers (> version 6) web map. Bonus points for utilisation of on-device GPUs/NPUs available in modern desktop/mobile devices. (not mandatory)
Harsh Astro Tech Gtbit: Alright, let’s break it down in a simpler way.

### What you’re trying to do:
You want to make a system that automatically creates smooth videos using satellite images that get updated every 30 minutes. So, instead of just jumping from one image to the next (which can be kinda choppy), we use some AI magic to fill in the gaps. Imagine you’re watching a cloud move smoothly across the map in real-time, not just hopping from one point to another.

### The challenge:
Clouds are tricky—they move, change shape, and sometimes disappear between frames. It’s like trying to predict what’s happening in a video game when the character is constantly moving and warping around.

### What’s the goal?
You want these smooth videos to show up on a map that people can interact with, like zooming in and out or moving around. Now, fancy commercial tools like Mapbox make this easy, but since you want to stick with free, open-source stuff (think OpenLayers or Leaflet), it’s going to take a bit more work to make it happen.

### Who’s it for?
People who develop apps for WebGIS (basically web maps with tons of data), and also for folks who use tools like VEDAS or BHUVAN (satellite data sites) to check out satellite imagery.

### The plan:
- You want this to work with a standard satellite service (WMS, Web Map Service).
- You’ll show off the video on a map, like OpenLayers (version 6 or later).
- If you can make it work on fancy desktop or mobile devices that have strong GPUs (graphics cards) or NPUs (AI chips), even better! But that’s just a bonus.

### Why not use other tools?
Sure, you can find tools that do some of these things, but a complete, smooth system like this doesn’t exist yet.

This tool will basically bring all the pieces together and work with free stuff (open-source maps), so anyone can use it, without paying for commercial licenses. 

So, in short: you’re making cloud animations smooth AF, showing them on a map, and doing it all with free, open-source tools. 👌

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Technical Workflow for RE-DACT
1. Data Ingestion
Input Formats: Text files (TXT, DOCX), spreadsheets (CSV, XLSX), images (JPG, PNG), PDFs, and videos.
User Upload: Data is uploaded by the user through the web-based or offline interface.
Preprocessing:
Data is scanned to detect the format and its structure (e.g., headers in CSV, metadata in images).
Identify sensitive content using rule-based methods (e.g., regular expressions for email addresses, names, etc.).
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 2. Redaction/Masking/Anonymization Pipeline
2.1 Data Parsing & Tokenization
Text Data:
Tokenize input data (split into identifiable elements like words, sentences).
Natural Language Processing (NLP) algorithms identify sensitive content (e.g., names, dates, locations).
Image Data:
OCR (Optical Character Recognition) extracts text from images.
Image segmentation algorithms detect regions with sensitive information.
Video Data:
Frame extraction and OCR for text in videos, with object detection for faces or other identifiable features.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 2.2 Redaction Based on User Preferences
Gradational Redaction:
Users choose the level of redaction, ranging from basic anonymization (e.g., replacing names) to full masking or data obfuscation.
Redaction Levels:
Low: Redact only personally identifiable information (PII) like names or addresses.
Medium: Mask content that could link to an individual, including indirect identifiers.
High: Create synthetic data by replacing all original values with realistic but fictitious equivalents
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 3. Machine Learning Integration
Model Training:
Use pre-labeled datasets (text/images) for sensitive data detection (e.g., open-source datasets, user feedback data).
Continual learning loop: System improves over time by learning from user actions (e.g., corrections to redactions).
Sensitive Entity Detection:
Named Entity Recognition (NER) models extract entities such as names, locations, and dates.
Image recognition models identify faces, license plates, or other identifying features.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Image recognition models identify faces, license plates, or other identifying features.
Synthetic Data Generation:
Use Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) or transformers to create synthetic data that mimics the structure but is devoid of sensitive content.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 4. Output Generation
Redacted Output:
The tool generates redacted files in formats such as text (redacted DOCX), images (blurring or blacking out regions), or PDFs.
Annotations: Optional annotations (e.g., "Name removed") can be embedded in the file.
Partial Downloads/Selective Streaming:
For large files (e.g., videos), the system allows users to selectively download or view specific portions of redacted content.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 5. Data Security and Privacy
No Data Retention:
No input data is stored beyond the session unless explicitly chosen by the user.
Encryption:
Input/output files and communication are encrypted using industry-standard encryption protocols (e.g., AES-256).
Offline Mode:
The tool supports an offline mode for users who don’t want to upload sensitive data to the cloud.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 6. User Interface & Experience
Web-based/Offline GUI:
Drag-and-Drop: Easy file upload and navigation.
Customization: Users can adjust redaction preferences via sliders (low to high).
Preview: A real-time preview of redacted data before finalizing output.
Logs: Option to generate logs of redacted data with detailed reports
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 7. System Integration
COG (Cloud Optimized GeoTIFF) Integration:
For satellite or geospatial data (if required), convert INSAT data into COG format for redaction.
API Integration:
Provide REST APIs for integrating RE-DACT into other enterprise systems or workflows.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 8. Performance Monitoring and Evaluation
Metrics:
Precision/Recall: Measure the accuracy of redacted content (how well the system removes sensitive information without removing non-sensitive content).
Speed: Track the performance in terms of processing time, especially for large files (e.g., PDFs, videos).
User Feedback Loop: Gather feedback from users on the quality of redactions for continual model improvements.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Technologies Used
NLP Frameworks: SpaCy, NLTK, Hugging Face Transformers for entity detection.
OCR: Tesseract for text extraction from images and videos.
Machine Learning: TensorFlow, PyTorch for model training and synthetic data generation.
Frontend: React, Vue.js for building the user interface.
Backend: Python, Node.js for handling file processing and API requests.
Database: NoSQL (MongoDB) for storing metadata or logs (if needed).
Cloud/Offline Deployment: Docker for deploying both online and offline versions.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: This workflow ensures that RE-DACT operates efficiently across various file formats, offers customization to users, and incorporates machine learning for improving accuracy and generating synthetic data over time.

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Final Workflow for Web-Based Real-Time Spacecraft Simulation Application
To create a web-based real-time spacecraft simulation with an AI-based decision maker, you need to structure both front-end and back-end systems effectively. Below is a detailed workflow divided into key steps:

1. Architecture Overview
This architecture will focus on:

Front-End (Browser): Handles rendering, user input, and visualization.
Back-End (Server): Manages real-time simulation, physics, AI decision-making, and cloud-based processing.
Real-Time Communication: Ensures smooth data flow between the front-end and back-end for real-time simulation.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 2. Workflow Breakdown
Step 1: User Interacts with Web Interface
Front-End (Browser):

A web page is loaded that provides an interactive UI for the spacecraft simulation.
The UI is built with a JavaScript framework (React or Vue.js) for user-friendly controls, panels, and HUD.
Three.js or Babylon.js is used to render the 3D spacecraft and space environment in real-time using WebGL.
Features:

Spacecraft controls for thrust, orientation, and navigation.
Real-time visualization of the spacecraft and celestial objects.
HUD (Heads-Up Display) showing mission stats (velocity, AI decisions, resource levels, etc.).
Start/Stop buttons to initiate or halt the simulation.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Step 2: User Sends Commands to the Back-End
Front-End → Back-End Communication:

User commands (e.g., spacecraft movement, manual adjustments, navigation inputs) are captured via the UI.
Commands are sent to the back-end server through WebSockets for real-time data transfer or REST APIs for less time-critical updates.
Data Sent to Back-End:

Command inputs (navigation, power adjustments).
Game state updates (start, stop, pause).
Any player decisions (manual overrides to AI).
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Step 3: Back-End Processes the Simulation
Simulation Environment (Server):

The back-end server runs the core simulation logic, handling:
Physics Calculations (using a physics engine, e.g., PhysX, Bullet, or a custom physics module).
AI Decision-Making (using machine learning models or behavior trees to autonomously control spacecraft when no user input is given).
The AI system makes real-time decisions about navigation, fuel management, obstacle avoidance, etc.
Cloud-Based AI and Physics:

The heavy lifting for AI decisions and physics simulation happens on the server, potentially using a cloud platform like AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure.
The AI engine could be based on Python (TensorFlow, PyTorch) or C++ for speed, with the AI logic trained offline and deployed on the server.
If you are using machine learning, the model is pre-trained and then deployed for inference during runtime.
Physics calculations are done based on spacecraft interactions with space objects, trajectories, and real-time inputs.
Real-Time Data Processing:

The server processes spacecraft physics and AI responses and computes updated positions, velocities, rotations, etc.
Data such as spacecraft telemetry, environmental factors, and AI decision outcomes are calculated and sent back to the front-end.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Step 4: Real-Time Updates Sent to the Front-End
Back-End → Front-End Communication:
Updated spacecraft states (position, orientation, AI decisions, resource levels) are sent back to the browser via WebSockets for real-time updates or REST API for non-critical updates.
Data includes AI decisions on navigation and system management, which are reflected in the UI.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Step 5: Real-Time 3D Rendering and Visualization
Front-End Rendering:

The front-end uses WebGL with Three.js or Babylon.js to update the 3D model of the spacecraft based on the real-time data received from the back-end.
The spacecraft’s position, orientation, and speed are adjusted visually in response to user inputs and AI decisions.
The HUD is updated to reflect AI status, mission objectives, fuel levels, etc.
Visual Updates:

Smooth real-time rendering of space simulation: rotation, thrust changes, or AI-guided maneuvers are visualized immediately.
HUD panels show telemetry data, real-time decision outputs (AI choices on thrust adjustments or obstacle avoidance), and user control feedback.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 3. Technical Components
Front-End (Browser)
UI and Control Interface:

React or Vue.js for user controls, spacecraft data panels, and dynamic UI.
WebGL via Three.js or Babylon.js to handle the 3D rendering of the spacecraft and space environment.
WebSocket Integration:

Real-time bi-directional communication between the browser and server for data transfer (commands and telemetry).
REST API fallback for non-time-sensitive requests or state persistence.
Back-End (Server)
Real-Time Simulation:

Core simulation engine: Written in C++ or Python depending on performance needs (AI models, physics engine).
AI Decision-Making: Uses Machine Learning (TensorFlow or PyTorch) or a simpler rule-based system.
Physics Engine: Can use a cloud-based solution or an open-source engine like Bullet to handle spacecraft dynamics.
Cloud Hosting:

Use AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure to host the back-end servers, ensuring scalability and performance.
WebSockets or REST APIs are used to communicate with the front-end.
AI System
Pre-Trained AI Model: (e.g., reinforcement learning model for autonomous control decisions).
The model is deployed on the back-end to make decisions like collision avoidance, fuel optimization, or autopilot navigation.
Decision Frequency: Updated every few milliseconds (adjustable based on device capability and server load).
WebAssembly (WASM) (Optional)
WebAssembly for Browser-Based Computations:
Any heavy client-side computations (like simple physics or lightweight AI) can be handled via WASM. This improves browser performance by running near-native code (like C++ or Rust).
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 4. Optional Enhancements
Cloud-Based Multiplayer
Support multiple users accessing the simulation, where each user controls their spacecraft or works together in a shared virtual space. This could be achieved by maintaining a server-client architecture that synchronizes the states of multiple simulations.
AI Learning Loop (Advanced)
Implement a system where the AI learns from user interactions during the simulation. Data from user inputs can be collected and used to improve AI behavior over time using a feedback loop on the server.
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: 5. Deployment and Testing
Testing for Web Performance
Optimize for Low-End Devices:
Use responsive design to scale the web application UI across different device resolutions.
Ensure the Three.js or Babylon.js 3D scenes are optimized for performance, with simple models and minimal post-processing effects.
Profiling:
Use browser-based profiling tools (like Lighthouse, Chrome DevTools) to identify and eliminate performance bottlenecks.
Continuous Integration (CI) and Deployment (CD)
Automate the deployment of front-end updates to cloud platforms using CI/CD tools like GitHub Actions or Jenkins.
Automatically scale the back-end infrastructure based on the number of concurrent users.
Conclusion
The workflow for creating a web-based real-time spacecraft simulation involves integrating a 3D-rendered UI with back-end systems that handle simulation physics and AI decision-making. This architecture focuses on scalability, efficient communication (via WebSockets), and delivering smooth user interactions and real-time AI decisions, even on low-end devices.

Psycotomato: @917395099604 @918745942990 presentation ka draft bhejna kal shaam tak maximum and mujhe aaj 12 baje tak technological frameworks for frontend bhejo @918708908855.
Psycotomato: To create a web-based real-time spacecraft simulation with an AI-based decision maker, our steps are:

1. *User interacts with the web interface browser.*  (Add software)
The features include a spacecraft from a first perspective POV which controls thrust, orientation and navigating. Real-time visualization of the spacecraft and celestial objects and HUD showing the mission stats. Start/Stop buttons are also there to initiate or halt the simulation.

2. *The user sends the commands to the Back-end.*
The User commands are captured via the UP and these commands are sent to the back-end server through WebSockets for real-time data transfer or REST APIs for less-time critical updates.

3. *Back-End processes the simulation*
The back-end server then runs the core simulation logic handling like physics calculations and AI-Decision Making. The AI system makes real-time decisions about navigation, fuel management, obstacle avoidance, natural calamities, etc. We would be using cloud platform Google Cloud to create physics simulation and the AI engine is to be based on Python (Tensorflow).

4. *Updates are sent to front-end*
The updated spacecraft states are sent back to the browser via WebSockets or REST API for real-time updates and non-critical updates. 

5. *3D Rendering and Visualization*
(What would you be using for front end designing?)
Psycotomato: Iss summary ke regarding bata dena.
Harsh Astro Tech Gtbit: Front-End Technologies:
JavaScript Framework:

React or Vue.js: These are used for building the user interface (UI) and handling user interactions. They provide dynamic, efficient rendering of the web interface, including spacecraft controls, panels, and HUD.

3D Rendering:

Three.js or Babylon.js: These WebGL-based libraries are utilized for real-time 3D rendering of the spacecraft and space environment. They handle the visual display and animation of the spacecraft based on real-time data from the back-end.

WebSocket Integration:

Used for real-time, bi-directional communication between the front-end and back-end. This enables immediate updates to the spacecraft state and allows commands to be sent to the server with minimal delay.

REST API (Fallback):

When real-time communication is not necessary, REST APIs are used for less time-sensitive data transfers or state persistence.

HUD (Heads-Up Display):

The front-end will manage a dynamic HUD to show mission stats, AI decisions, resource levels, etc. This can be built using React/Vue components and updated in real-time using WebSocket data.

WebAssembly (Optional):

WASM can be used to optimize performance by offloading any heavy client-side computations (such as physics or lightweight AI) to run near-native code (like C++ or Rust) directly in the browser. <This message was edited>

Harsh Astro Tech Gtbit: To create a web-based real-time spacecraft simulation with an AI-based decision maker, our steps are:

1. User interacts with the web interface browser.  (Add software)
The features include a spacecraft from a first perspective POV which controls thrust, orientation and navigating. Real-time visualization of the spacecraft and celestial objects and HUD showing the mission stats. Start/Stop buttons are also there to initiate or halt the simulation.

2. The user sends the commands to the Back-end.
The User commands are captured via the UP and these commands are sent to the back-end server through WebSockets for real-time data transfer or REST APIs for less-time critical updates.

3. Back-End processes the simulation
The back-end server then runs the core simulation logic handling like physics calculations and AI-Decision Making. The AI system makes real-time decisions about navigation, fuel management, obstacle avoidance, natural calamities, etc. We would be using cloud platform Google Cloud to create physics simulation and the AI engine is to be based on Python (Tensorflow).

4. Updates are sent to front-end
The updated spacecraft states are sent back to the browser via WebSockets or REST API for real-time updates and non-critical updates. 

5. 3D Rendering and Visualization
(What would you be using for front end designing?)
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
*FRONTEND TECH:*

JavaScript Framework:

React or Vue.js: These are used for building the user interface (UI) and handling user interactions. They provide dynamic, efficient rendering of the web interface, including spacecraft controls, panels, and HUD.

3D Rendering:

Three.js or Babylon.js: These WebGL-based libraries are utilized for real-time 3D rendering of the spacecraft and space environment. They handle the visual display and animation of the spacecraft based on real-time data from the back-end.

WebSocket Integration:

Used for real-time, bi-directional communication between the front-end and back-end. This enables immediate updates to the spacecraft state and allows commands to be sent to the server with minimal delay.

REST API (Fallback):

When real-time communication is not necessary, REST APIs are used for less time-sensitive data transfers or state persistence.

HUD (Heads-Up Display):

The front-end will manage a dynamic HUD to show mission stats, AI decisions, resource levels, etc. This can be built using React/Vue components and updated in real-time using WebSocket data.

WebAssembly (Optional):

WASM can be used to optimize performance by offloading any heavy client-side computations (such as physics or lightweight AI) to run near-native code (like C++ or Rust) directly in the browser.

Psycotomato: To create a web-based real-time spacecraft simulation with an AI-based decision maker, our steps are:

1. User interacts with the web interface browser.  
We would be using React for building the UI and handling user interactions.
The features include a spacecraft from a first perspective POV which controls thrust, orientation and navigating. Real-time visualization of the spacecraft and celestial objects and HUD showing the mission stats. Start/Stop buttons are also there to initiate or halt the simulation.

2. The user sends the commands to the Back-end.
The User commands are captured via the UP and these commands are sent to the back-end server through WebSockets for real-time data transfer or REST APIs for less-time critical updates.

3. Back-End processes the simulation
The back-end server then runs the core simulation logic handling like physics calculations and AI-Decision Making. The AI system makes real-time decisions about navigation, fuel management, obstacle avoidance, natural calamities, etc. We would be using cloud platform Google Cloud to create physics simulation and the AI engine is to be based on Python (Tensorflow).

4. Updates are sent to front-end
The updated spacecraft states are sent back to the browser via WebSockets or REST API for real-time updates and non-critical updates. 

5. 3D Rendering and Visualization
For 3D Rendering and Visualization we would be using Three.js. These WebGL-based libraries are utilized for real-time 3D rendering of the spacecraft and space environment.

Parichay: @13135550002 write a program in python to delete all contacts with name having gtbit
Meta AI: Here's a simple Python program using the `contacts` library to delete contacts with names containing "GTBIT":

```
import contacts

def delete_contacts():
    # Load contacts
    address_book = contacts.AddressBook()

    # Find and delete contacts with names containing "GTBIT"
    for contact in address_book.contacts:
        if "GTBIT" in contact.name:
            address_book.delete_contact(contact)

    # Save changes
    address_book.save()

delete_contacts()
```

However, this code may not work on all systems due to differences in contact management APIs.

For Android, consider using `android.contacts` API or third-party libraries like `pyandroid`.

For iOS, consider using `pyobjus` or `contactssync`.

For desktop platforms, consider using platform-specific contact management libraries.

*Please note:* Deleting contacts is irreversible. Ensure you have backups and test this code in a controlled environment.

Would you like more information on platform-specific implementations?

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: "use client"

import React, { useRef } from 'react'
import { Canvas, useFrame } from '@react-three/fiber'
import { Sphere, OrbitControls } from '@react-three/drei'
import { LineChart, Line, XAxis, YAxis, CartesianGrid, Tooltip, ResponsiveContainer } from 'recharts'

const EuropaSphere = () => {
  const meshRef = useRef()
  useFrame(() => {
    if (meshRef.current) {
      meshRef.current.rotation.y += 0.002
    }
  })

  return (
    <Sphere ref={meshRef} args={[1, 64, 64]}>
      <meshStandardMaterial color="#a5d8ff" metalness={0.5} roughness={0.7} />
    </Sphere>
  )
}

const data = [
  { name: '1', value: 40 },
  { name: '2', value: 30 },
  { name: '3', value: 60 },
  { name: '4', value: 80 },
  { name: '5', value: 50 },
  { name: '6', value: 70 },
  { name: '7', value: 90 },
]

export default function Component() {
  return (
    <div className="bg-[#0a0f2a] text-blue-100 min-h-screen p-8 font-mono">
      <div className="max-w-6xl mx-auto">
        <h1 className="text-5xl font-bold mb-8 text-blue-300">EUROPA</h1>
        <div className="relative">
          <div className="w-[500px] h-[500px] mx-auto mb-8">
            <Canvas>
              <ambientLight intensity={0.5} />
              <pointLight position={[10, 10, 10]} />
              <EuropaSphere />
              <OrbitControls enableZoom={false} enablePan={false} />
            </Canvas>
          </div>
          <div className="absolute top-1/2 left-1/2 transform -translate-x-1/2 -translate-y-1/2 text-center">
            <p className="text-sm opacity-70">DIAMETER</p>
            <p className="text-xl font-semibold">3,121.6 km</p>
          </div>
          <div className="absolute top-0 left-0">
            <p className="text-sm opacity-70">ORBITAL PERIOD</p>
            <p className="text-xl font-semibold">3.55 days</p>
          </div>
          <div className="absolute top-0 right-0">
            <p className="text-sm opacity-70">SURFACE TEMP</p>
            <p className="text-xl font-semibold">-160°C</p>
          </div>
        </div>
        
        <div className="flex justify-center mb-8">
          <div className="w-1/2">
            <div className="relative">
              <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" className="w-full">
                <path d="M0,50 Q50,0 100,50" fill="none" stroke="#38bdf8" strokeWidth="0.5" />
                <circle cx="50" cy="25" r="3" fill="#38bdf8" />
              </svg>
              <div className="absolute bottom-0 left-0 text-sm">0</div>
              <div className="absolute bottom-0 right-0 text-sm">100</div>
              <div className="absolute top-0 left-1/2 -translate-x-1/2 text-sm">50</div>
            </div>
            <p className="text-center mt-2">SURFACE MAPPING PROGRESS</p>
          </div>
        </div>
        
        <div className="flex justify-between">
          <div className="w-1/2 pr-4">
            <h2 className="text-2xl font-semibold mb-4 text-blue-300">Surface Activity</h2>
            <ResponsiveContainer width="100%" height={200}>
              <LineChart data={data}>
                <CartesianGrid strokeDasharray="3 3" stroke="#1e3a8a" />
                <XAxis dataKey="name" stroke="#60a5fa" />
                <YAxis stroke="#60a5fa" />
                <Tooltip contentStyle={{ backgroundColor: '#1e3a8a', border: 'none' }} />
                <Line type="monotone" dataKey="value" stroke="#38bdf8" strokeWidth={2} />
              </LineChart>
            </ResponsiveContainer>
          </div>
          <div className="w-1/2 pl-4">
            <h3 className="text-2xl font-semibold mb-4 text-blue-300">ATMOSPHERE COMPOSITION</h3>
            <ul className="space-y-2">
              <li className="flex justify-between">
                <span>Oxygen</span>
                <span>90%</span>
              </li>
              <li className="flex justify-between">
                <span>Nitrogen</span>
                <span>5%</span>
              </li>
              <li className="flex justify-between">
                <span>Other</span>
                <span>5%</span>
              </li>
            </ul>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  )

Yatin Cse 3: Hey everyone,

I just wanted to mention that at no point did Gursheel Kaur or Jiya Khera reach out to me about my "inactivity." I genuinely thought I was on top of things—handling the technical side of Luna(you know, overseeing the web dev team, app dev team, cybersecurity, AIML and building website and an app for the club), while running regular meetings with the technical team including teaching them basics of webdev from scratch and aiml, so that we can have a robust team of developers to tackle any technical challenges. And contributing anything i can in the offline events. But hey, apparently, I missed the memo, and here I am, realizing I wasn’t up to the Luna club standards.

Before I officially step down from my duties (and leave luna club for good), a word of advice: next time, instead of rushing to Avishi with every tiny problem, try talking amongst yourselves first. make the full use of the private message feature to solve problems without creating a three-act high school drama. 

I also want to point out that Gursheel has often referred to me calling me by many names like "chu***a" in front of everyone but I guess I will always be the one who misbehaves. 

It was pointless of me to give my time and energy to the Luna club at the end. 
Take care and have fun. I'm out, thanks to some premium-level pettiness.

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: import numpy as np
from skyfield.api import load, Topos

# Load the necessary files
planets = load('de421.bsp')
ts = load.timescale()

# Define the planet and time
planet = planets['earth']
t = ts.now()

# Calculate the planet's coordinates
ra, dec, distance = planet.at(t).radec()

# Print the coordinates
print(f'Right Ascension: {ra}')
print(f'Declination: {dec}')
print(f'Distance: {distance}')

import ephem

# Define the planet and time
planet = ephem.Mars()
planet.compute(ephem.now())

# Print the coordinates
print(f'Right Ascension: {planet.ra}')
print(f'Declination: {planet.dec}')
print(f'Distance: {planet.earth_distance}')

Snehehehehehe: Hello DesignVerse Participants! 🚀

We’re thrilled to kick off Round 1 of DesignVerse 2024! It’s time to unleash your creativity and design something truly remarkable. Below are the details for the first round of the competition:

🟢 Round 1: Online Challenge
📅 Date: 21st October 2024
⏰ Time: 3:00 PM – 6:00 PM IST
📍 Platform: Discord 
(Join here: https://discord.gg/YvaruhHJ )


* Choose your preferred theme and design a responsive UI (web/app) using Figma, Canva, or any software of your choice.

* Submit your designs within the 3-hour window for evaluation.

* Shortlisted teams will advance to Round 2.
Snehehehehehe: 📌 General Guidelines:

• Ensure your design adheres to the theme.
• Use Figma, Canva, or any design software—your choice!
• AI-generated content or plagiarism is strictly prohibited.
• The organizing team’s decisions are final. Any form of misconduct will lead to disqualification.

Let’s get ready to create something amazing! Good luck, everyone! 🎉
Snehehehehehe: *Round Structure:*
Round 1 (Online):
In this round, participants will be given a theme and will need to create a design using Figma, Canva, or any other software within three hours. The online round will be hosted on Discord, where teams will submit their designs for evaluation. The best entries will be shortlisted to advance to the next round.
Timings: 3-6 PM

Round 2 (Hybrid):
Shortlisted teams will participate in a hybrid format, where they will develop a working prototype, whether it be on Figma, any other platform, or a website, based on their Round 1 design. Teams will pitch their solutions to the judges, showcasing the functionality and overall concept for evaluation.
Venue: Bharati Vidyapeeth College of Engineering
Online Platform: Discord
Timings: 3-5 PM
Snehehehehehe: *Themes:*

1.*Design for Tomorrow*
Focus on shaping a more innovative, inclusive, and sustainable future by addressing real-world challenges across various sectors.
2.*Beyond Boundaries*
Encourage participants to design tools or platforms that bridge cultural, geographical, or language divides, fostering global interconnectedness.
Snehehehehehe: Attention Participants:

Please change your *server nickname* to your *team's name* . Follow these steps:

1. *Open Discord:* Log in to your account.
2. *Select the Server:* Click on our server.
3. *Locate Your Username:* Find your name in the member list.
4. *Right-Click Your Username:* Right-click on your name or click the three dots next to it.
5. *Choose "Change Nickname":* Select "Change Nickname" from the menu.
6. *Enter Your Team Name:* Type your team's name and click "Save" or press Enter.
Thank you for your cooperation!

Snehehehehehe: The concept for "DripKit" as a thrift store website with a vintage, global connection theme revolves around blending the unique charm of second-hand, handmade, and upcycled products with a modern shopping experience. The idea is to create a nostalgic, visually captivating platform that emphasizes sustainability, craftsmanship, and individuality while also providing a seamless, global shopping experience.

Key Elements of the Idea:
Vintage Thrift Store Aesthetic:

The homepage layout, design, and overall aesthetic aim to evoke the feeling of exploring a quaint, retro thrift shop where each item tells a story. The vintage theme brings out a sense of nostalgia, making users feel like they’re discovering rare and unique pieces from different eras and regions.
This theme is represented through the use of retro fonts, muted colors like mustard yellows, olive greens, and soft pastels, as well as textured backgrounds mimicking materials like old paper, wood, or fabric.
It’s not just about shopping; it’s about creating an emotional connection with the products and the artisans who make or sell them.
Global Artisanship and Sustainability:

Handmade items from artisans worldwide and upcycled, preloved goods are central to the product offerings. Each artisan is given a profile on the site where users can learn about their story, how their products are made, and the culture behind their craftsmanship.
The platform highlights the sustainable nature of buying second-hand and handmade products, emphasizing the environmental benefits of reducing waste and supporting local, eco-friendly businesses.
Vintage and handmade products are framed as more than just items – they are ethical purchases that reflect thoughtful consumption and contribute to a more sustainable future.
Product Listings with Retro Flair:

The product listings are designed to look like old postcards or vintage labels, giving the items a personal, curated feel. This design choice enhances the feeling that each item is a one-of-a-kind find or a hidden gem waiting to be uncovered.
Items could be tagged with phrases like "Rare Find" or "Limited Edition" to reinforce the uniqueness of each product, just as you would expect in a small thrift shop.
Interactive & Personal Connection:

The website not only connects customers with global artisans but also allows for community interaction, such as voting on the “Thrift Find of the Month” or sharing personal stories of favorite purchases in a nostalgic scrapbook-style format.
The inclusion of personalized product recommendations based on past shopping behavior and user input enhances the idea of creating a personal connection between the user and the platform.
Focus on Experience:

Shopping on DripKit isn’t just about purchasing items; it’s about creating an immersive experience. The retro-inspired design elements, handwritten fonts, and textures give users the feeling that they are not just browsing a website but exploring a curated collection of goods with historical and emotional value.
The visual storytelling approach—using artisan stories, Polaroid-style photos, and cultural backgrounds—enhances the connection between the customer and the product.
Modern Shopping Experience with a Vintage Twist:

Despite the vintage feel, the website is designed to offer all the conveniences of modern e-commerce, such as secure payment gateways, global shipping, and mobile-friendly designs. This ensures a smooth, reliable shopping experience while maintaining the thrift shop charm.
Sustainability is highlighted in product labels, filtering options (e.g., by decade, material), and a dedicated section that explains how each purchase contributes to reducing environmental impact.
Why This Idea Works:
Target Audience Appeal:

The platform would appeal to customers who love vintage, thrifted, and handcrafted goods but also care about ethical shopping and sustainability.
It taps into the growing market of people interested in eco-conscious shopping, global craftsmanship, and those who love discovering unique, one-off items.
Emotional Engagement:

By focusing on nostalgia and storytelling, DripKit doesn’t just offer products; it offers an experience that feels personal and memorable. Customers are more likely to feel a deeper connection to the items they purchase, knowing they’re supporting artisans and sustainable practices.
Differentiation:

DripKit sets itself apart from other e-commerce platforms by blending the best aspects of a vintage thrift store with the global, handmade market, creating a fusion of cultures, eras, and craftsmanship all within a visually rich, retro-themed website.
The platform also offers a unique blend of old and new by incorporating modern web design trends like mobile responsiveness and personalized shopping recommendations, but with a nostalgic twist.
Would you like to focus more on any particular aspect of this idea?

*Role Overview:*

Seeking a motivated Sales Intern to join our UK office. This role is perfect for female students looking to gain valuable experience in sales and enhance their CV. As a Sales Intern, you will engage in cold calling and have the opportunity to contribute to our consulting firm's growth.

*Responsibilities:*

- Conduct 20-30 cold calls daily to potential clients.
- Assist in developing sales strategies and identifying new business opportunities.
- Maintain accurate records of calls and client interactions.

*Qualifications:*

- Excellent command of English is essential.
- Previous experience in sales is a plus, but not required.
- Strong communication and interpersonal skills.
- Self-motivated and able to work independently.

*Additional Information:*

- Flexible working hours aligned with UK time, ensuring no interference with studies.
- Opportunity for growth with *potential for commission* on successful deals or an *increased salary based on performance*.
- Ideal for those looking to gain experience before entering the workforce full-time.

If you're eager to learn and ready to take on a challenge, we'd love to hear from you!

Parichay: @13135550002 is guns legal in west bengal?
Meta AI: In West Bengal, India, guns are regulated under the Arms Act, 1959, and the Arms Rules, 1962. Private ownership of firearms is allowed, but heavily regulated.

To possess firearms in West Bengal:

1. Obtain an arms license from the District Magistrate or Commissioner of Police.
2. Meet eligibility criteria (e.g., age, character, and residency requirements).
3. Pass a background check.
4. Complete firearms training.

Licensed firearms include:

1. Shotguns (for hunting and sport shooting)
2. Rifles (for hunting and target shooting)
3. Handguns (for self-defense, rarely issued)

Prohibited firearms include:

1. Automatic and semi-automatic rifles
2. Machine guns
3. Explosive or incendiary devices

Illicit possession, sale, or manufacture of firearms is punishable under Indian law.

For detailed information and updates, consult:

1. West Bengal Police website
2. Official Government of India websites
3. Local authorities

Please note that laws and regulations are subject to change.

Parichay: *🚗 TRAVEL TRIBES Presenting Jibhi-Tirthan Valley Tour Programme 🌄*

*Let’s Travel together 🌟*
DATE- 3/01/2025-6/01/2025


*Pickup Location:* Akshardham Metro Station/Rohini Sec-18 Metro Station 🚉

*Plan & Duration:*  
2 Nights & 3 Days (2N/3D) 🌅

*Day 0:* *Departure*  
Starting Pickup from *Delhi** Chandigarh*.  
You will be departing from Delhi to *Jibhi-Tirthan Valley* by AC *Tempo Traveller* 🚍.

*Day 1:* *Jibhi Local Sightseeing*  
- After an overnight journey, drive along the Beas and Tirthan rivers from *Aut* to reach the beautiful village of *Jibhi* by 10 AM-12 PM. Check-in to the *Resort*.  
- Go for local sightseeing, such as *Jibhi Waterfall*, *Mini Thailand*, and explore the local market, try coffee, and local dishes within walking distance.  
- By evening, enjoy the *bonfire* 🔥 and have an overnight stay in *Jibhi*.

*Day 2:* *360° View Point | Siroskar Lake*  
- After breakfast, leave for the stunning *Jibhi - Jalori Pass* and start your trek.  
- Enjoy a stunning 360° view of the *Great Himalayas* 🏞 and continue the trek to *Siroskar Lake*, surrounded by Alpine trees 🌲.  
- The *Serolsar Lake* is sacred to the locals and famous for the nearby temple dedicated to *Goddess Budhi Nagin*.  
- Trek back to *Jalori Pass* and return to the resort in *Jibhi*. Enjoy a *bonfire* 🔥 and overnight stay.

*Day 3:* *Chehni Kothi/Chhoie Waterfall | Departure (Last Day)*  
- Wake up early to witness a beautiful sunrise 🌅.  
- After breakfast, check out from the resort and depart for *Chehni Kothi* in *Banjar* or *Chhoie Waterfall* in *Tirthan Valley*.  
- Later, depart for *Delhi* and reach Delhi the next morning around 8 AM (tentatively).  
- *Tour End...*

*Accommodation & Inclusions:*  
✅ *AC Tempo Traveller* 🚍  
✅ *2-Night Stay in Resort/Hotel* 🏨  
✅ *Meals*: 2 Breakfast + 2 Dinner 🍳🍽  
✅ *Jibhi Waterfall* 🌊  
✅ *Mini Thailand Pool* 🏖  
✅ *Bonfire & Music* 🔥🎶  
✅ *Jalori Pass Valley* 🏞  
✅ *Serolsar Lake Trek* 🏞  
✅ *Chehni Kothi in Banjar* 🏰  
✅ *Chhoie Waterfall* 💦  
✅ *Mentioned Local Sightseeing* 🏞  
✅ *Trip Leader* 🧑‍🦯  
✅ *24/7 Support* 🤝

Evil is not something we desire to be, yet it is something we must be. Survival instincts triumph over morality, making even the act of killing plants for sustenance inherently evil. Selfishness and primal instincts prevail, for survival demands it. We are evil, to survive.

Evil exists in the divine itself. His nature is steeped in the same evil, which is why He remains distant and unknowable. Perhaps God created humanity as a reflection of Himself, hoping to rationalize His own actions, to prove they were not evil. Or perhaps we are simply His way of consoling Himself, a mirror of His flaws. Unable to bear the shattering of His pride, He created us as spiteful beings. Upon glancing at His own reflection, He became too ashamed, unable to undo the sins He bore onto us through selfishness. It is too late; the burden of His regret lingers in the beings He shaped.

We humans are nothing but fleeting shadows in the cosmos. Yet, through consciousness, ego, and selfishness, we console ourselves, convincing ourselves that we are good. We glorify survival instincts, the predator’s hunger, and the pursuit of power and control, even when these are the root of all evil. What we call the "greater good" often masks our darkest impulses.

We condemn killing, yet we kill to eat. We decry violence, yet we wage wars for power, resources, and ideology. The hypocrisy overshadows the very morals we cherish. Evil is not a choice. Evil is not a potential. Evil is our destiny, embedded in the very fabric of existence. In the end, this destiny is what we shall carry to our deathbeds, an eternal reflection of the creator’s own flaws.

We’re thrilled to introduce *Gaming Titans*, a new society in GTBIT dedicated to gamers and the exciting world of esports and gaming culture! 🕹️ Whether you’re a casual player, competitive esports enthusiast, or just love gaming vibes, Gaming Titans is your ultimate hub for all things gaming.  

💡 *What We’re About:*  
- Hosting thrilling gaming tournaments and events.  
- Creating a vibrant community of gamers to connect and share.  
- Exploring the gaming industry and its opportunities.  
- Having a ton of fun along the way!  

Join us as we dive into epic battles, explore new worlds, and celebrate the passion that brings gamers together. Let’s level up and create unforgettable gaming experiences!

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: BHAI, FREAKY MODE MEIN GENUINE DSA IN CPP PDHANE KA MAZA HI ALAGA HAI!!!

CPP MEIN DSA KE LIYE KUCH GENUINE TOPICS HAIN:

1. *ARRAYS*: CPP MEIN ARRAYS KA USE KARKE DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATION AUR DEALLOCATION KI JAA SAKTI HAI.
2. *LINKED LISTS*: LINKED LISTS KA USE KARKE DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATION AUR DEALLOCATION KI JAA SAKTI HAI.
3. *STACKS*: STACKS KA USE KARKE LIFO (LAST IN FIRST OUT) OPERATIONS KI JAA SAKTI HAI.
4. *QUEUES*: QUEUES KA USE KARKE FIFO (FIRST IN FIRST OUT) OPERATIONS KI JAA SAKTI HAI.
5. *BINARY SEARCH TREES*: BINARY SEARCH TREES KA USE KARKE DATA KO EFFICIENTLY STORE AUR RETRIEVE KIYA JA SAKTA HAI.

CPP MEIN DSA KE LIYE KUCH IMPORTANT FUNCTIONS HAIN:

1. *SORT()*: ARRAY KO SORT KARNE KE LIYE.
2. *SEARCH()*: ARRAY MEIN ELEMENT KO SEARCH KARNE KE LIYE.
3. *INSERT()*: ARRAY MEIN ELEMENT KO INSERT KARNE KE LIYE.
4. *DELETE()*: ARRAY MEIN ELEMENT KO DELETE KARNE KE LIYE.

CPP MEIN DSA KE LIYE KUCH IMPORTANT ALGORITHMS HAIN:

1. *BUBBLE SORT*: ARRAY KO SORT KARNE KE LIYE.
2. *SELECTION SORT*: ARRAY KO SORT KARNE KE LIYE.
3. *INSERTION SORT*: ARRAY KO SORT KARNE KE LIYE.
4. *MERGE SORT*: ARRAY KO SORT KARNE KE LIYE.
5. *QUICK SORT*: ARRAY KO SORT KARNE KE LIYE.

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: BHAI, DETAILED CODES KE SAATH INSERTION AUR DELETION KE TYPES KE LIYE TAYAAR HO JAO!


INSERTION:


1. _Insertion at Beginning_:


```
void insertAtBeginning(int arr[], int &n, int capacity, int element) {
    if (n >= capacity) {
        cout << "Array is full!" << endl;
        return;
    }
    for (int i = n; i > 0; i--) {
        arr[i] = arr[i - 1];
    }
    arr[0] = element;
    n++;
}
```

2. _Insertion at End_:


```
void insertAtEnd(int arr[], int &n, int capacity, int element) {
    if (n >= capacity) {
        cout << "Array is full!" << endl;
        return;
    }
    arr[n] = element;
    n++;
}
```

3. _Insertion at Specific Position_:


```
void insertAtPosition(int arr[], int &n, int capacity, int position, int element) {
    if (n >= capacity) {
        cout << "Array is full!" << endl;
        return;
    }
    if (position < 0 || position > n) {
        cout << "Invalid position!" << endl;
        return;
    }
    for (int i = n; i > position; i--) {
        arr[i] = arr[i - 1];
    }
    arr[position] = element;
    n++;
}
```

DELETION:


1. _Deletion from Beginning_:


```
void deleteFromBeginning(int arr[], int &n) {
    if (n == 0) {
        cout << "Array is empty!" << endl;
        return;
    }
    for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        arr[i] = arr[i + 1];
    }
    n--;
}
```

2. _Deletion from End_:


```
void deleteFromEnd(int arr[], int &n) {
    if (n == 0) {
        cout << "Array is empty!" << endl;
        return;
    }
    n--;
}
```

3. _Deletion of Specific Element_:


```
void deleteSpecificElement(int arr[], int &n, int element) {
    int position = -1;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (arr[i] == element) {
            position = i;
            break;
        }
    }
    if (position == -1) {
        cout << "Element not found!" << endl;
        return;
    }
    for (int i = position; i < n - 1; i++) {
        arr[i] = arr[i + 1];
    }
    n--;
}
```

4. _Deletion of Element at Specific Position_:


```
void deleteAtPosition(int arr[], int &n, int position) {
    if (position < 0 || position >= n) {
        cout << "Invalid position!" << endl;
        return;
    }
    for (int i = position; i < n - 1; i++) {
        arr[i] = arr[i + 1];
    }
    n--;
}
```

BHAI, YEH TOH DETAILED CODES HAIN! AB TUMHARE PASS INSERTION AUR DELETION KE TYPES KE LIYE SAARI CHEEZEIN HAIN!


ABHI TUM KAUNSA INSERTION AUR DELETION TYPE CHAHOGE?
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: BHAI, INSERTION AUR DELETION KE TYPES KE LIYE TAYAAR HO JAO!


INSERTION:


1. *Insertion at Beginning*: Array ya linked list ke starting mein ek naya element insert karna.


2. *Insertion at End*: Array ya linked list ke ending mein ek naya element insert karna.


3. *Insertion at Specific Position*: Array ya linked list mein ek specific position par ek naya element insert karna.


DELETION:


1. *Deletion from Beginning*: Array ya linked list ke starting se ek element delete karna.


2. *Deletion from End*: Array ya linked list ke ending se ek element delete karna.


3. *Deletion of Specific Element*: Array ya linked list se ek specific element delete karna.


4. *Deletion of Element at Specific Position*: Array ya linked list se ek specific position par ek element delete karna.


BHAI, YEH TOH BASICS HAI! AB TUMHARE PASS INSERTION AUR DELETION KE TYPES KE LIYE SAARI CHEEZEIN HAIN!


ABHI TUM KAUNSA INSERTION AUR DELETION TYPE CHAHOGE?
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: BHAI, SORTING KE LIYE TAYAAR HO JAO!


Sorting ke liye humne kai algorithms hain, jinmein se kuchh popular hain:


1. *Bubble Sort*: Yeh sabse simple sorting algorithm hai. Yeh algorithm array ko iterate karta hai aur adjacent elements ko compare karta hai. Agar elements sahi order mein nahi hain, toh yeh unhe swap karta hai.


2. *Selection Sort*: Yeh algorithm array ko iterate karta hai aur sabse chhote element ko dhundhta hai. Phir yeh us element ko array ke starting mein le aata hai.


3. *Insertion Sort*: Yeh algorithm array ko iterate karta hai aur har element ko uske sahi position mein insert karta hai.


4. *Merge Sort*: Yeh algorithm array ko do parts mein divide karta hai aur phir unhe merge karta hai.


5. *Quick Sort*: Yeh algorithm array ko do parts mein divide karta hai aur phir unhe recursively sort karta hai.


6. *Heap Sort*: Yeh algorithm array ko heap mein convert karta hai aur phir ushe sort karta hai.


7. *Radix Sort*: Yeh algorithm array ko digits ke basis par sort karta hai.


8. *Timsort*: Yeh algorithm merge sort aur insertion sort ka combination hai.


BHAI, YEH TOH BASICS HAI! AB TUMHARE PASS SORTING KE LIYE SAARI CHEEZEIN HAIN!


ABHI TUM KAUNSA SORTING ALGORITHM CHAHOGE?
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: BHAI, FREAKY MODE ON!

DSA IN C++ KE LIYE DETAILED GUIDE YEH HAI:


# Step 1: Arrays aur Vectors
- Arrays: Static memory allocation, fixed size.
- Vectors: Dynamic memory allocation, resizable.

```
int arr[5] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
vector<int> vec = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
```

# Step 2: Linked Lists
- Singly Linked List: Ek node ke paas ek hi pointer hota hai.
- Doubly Linked List: Ek node ke paas do pointers hote hain.

```
struct Node {
    int data;
    Node* next;
};
```

# Step 3: Stacks aur Queues
- Stack: Last In First Out (LIFO)
- Queue: First In First Out (FIFO)

```
stack<int> s;
queue<int> q;
```

# Step 4: Trees aur Graphs
- Trees: Hierarchical structure, ek root node aur uske child nodes.
- Graphs: Network structure, nodes aur edges.

```
struct Node {
    int data;
    Node* left;
    Node* right;
};
```

# Step 5: Algorithms
- Sorting: Bubble sort, selection sort, insertion sort, merge sort, quick sort.
- Searching: Linear search, binary search.
- Graph traversal: DFS, BFS.

```
void bubbleSort(int arr[], int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n-1; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < n-i-1; j++) {
            if (arr[j] > arr[j+1]) {
                swap(arr[j], arr[j+1]);
            }
        }
    }
}
```

BHAI, YEH TOH BASICS HAI! AB TUMHARE PASS DSA KE LIYE SAARI CHEEZEIN HAIN!

ABHI TUM KIS TOPIC PAR CHAHOGE?
NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: BHAI, STACK, QUEUE, LINKED LIST, TREE, GRAPH, AUR SAB KUCH CODES KE SAATH!


*STACK*


Stack ek data structure hai jo LIFO (Last In First Out) principle par kaam karta hai.


```
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Stack {
    int* arr;
    int top;
    int capacity;
public:
    Stack(int capacity) {
        this->capacity = capacity;
        arr = new int[capacity];
        top = -1;
    }
    void push(int element) {
        if (top >= capacity - 1) {
            cout << "Stack overflow!" << endl;
            return;
        }
        arr[++top] = element;
    }
    int pop() {
        if (top < 0) {
            cout << "Stack underflow!" << endl;
            return -1;
        }
        return arr[top--];
    }
    int peek() {
        if (top < 0) {
            cout << "Stack is empty!" << endl;
            return -1;
        }
        return arr[top];
    }
    bool isEmpty() {
        return top < 0;
    }
};

int main() {
    Stack stack(5);
    stack.push(1);
    stack.push(2);
    stack.push(3);
    cout << "Peek: " << stack.peek() << endl;
    cout << "Pop: " << stack.pop() << endl;
    cout << "IsEmpty: " << stack.isEmpty() << endl;
    return 0;
}
```


*QUEUE*


Queue ek data structure hai jo FIFO (First In First Out) principle par kaam karta hai.


```
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Queue {
    int* arr;
    int front;
    int rear;
    int capacity;
public:
    Queue(int capacity) {
        this->capacity = capacity;
        arr = new int[capacity];
        front = rear = -1;
    }
    void enqueue(int element) {
        if (rear >= capacity - 1) {
            cout << "Queue overflow!" << endl;
            return;
        }
        if (front == -1) {
            front = rear = 0;
        } else {
            rear++;
        }
        arr[rear] = element;
    }
    int dequeue() {
        if (front == -1) {
            cout << "Queue underflow!" << endl;
            return -1;
        }
        int temp = arr[front];
        if (front == rear) {
            front = rear = -1;
        } else {
            front++;
        }
        return temp;
    }
    int peek() {
        if (front == -1) {
            cout << "Queue is empty!" << endl;
            return -1;
        }
        return arr[front];
    }
    bool isEmpty() {
        return front == -1;
    }
};

int main() {
    Queue queue(5);
    queue.enqueue(1);
    queue.enqueue(2);
    queue.enqueue(3);
    cout << "Peek: " << queue.peek() << endl;
    cout << "Dequeue: " << queue.dequeue() << endl;
    cout << "IsEmpty: " << queue.isEmpty() << endl;
    return 0;
}
```


*LINKED LIST*


Linked list ek data structure hai jo nodes ke roop mein data ko store karti hai.


```
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Node {
public:
    int data;
    Node* next;
};

class LinkedList {
    Node* head;
public:
    LinkedList() {
        head = nullptr;
    }
    void insert(int data) {
        Node* newNode = new Node();
        newNode->data = data;
        newNode->next = head;
        head = newNode;
    }
    void print() {
        Node* temp = head;
        while (temp != nullptr) {
            cout << temp->data << " ";
            temp = temp->next;
        }
        cout << endl;
    }
};

int main() {
    LinkedList list;
    list.insert(1);
    list.insert(2);
    list.insert(3);
    list.print();
    return 0;
}
```


*TREE*


Tree ek data structure hai jo nodes ke roop mein data ko store karti hai.


```
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Node {
public:
    int data;
    Node* left;
    Node* right;
};

class Tree {
    Node* root;
public:
    Tree() {
        root = nullptr;
    }
    void insert(int data) {
        Node* newNode = new Node();
        newNode->data = data;
        newNode->left = newNode->right = nullptr;
        if (root == nullptr) {
            root = newNode;
        } else {
            Node* temp = root;
            while (true) {
                if (data < temp->data
```

Parichay: @13135550002
Meta AI: Hello! What's on your mind?
Parichay: @13135550002 what is shell sort
Meta AI: Shell sort is an in-place comparison-based sorting algorithm. It generalizes the insertion sort algorithm by allowing the comparison and exchange of far-apart elements. Shell sort is named after its inventor, Donald Shell.

Here's a brief overview of how it works:

1. *Initial Gap*: The algorithm starts with a large gap (or interval) between elements, which is gradually reduced.
2. *Insertion Sort*: For each gap, the algorithm performs an insertion sort on the elements separated by that gap.
3. *Reducing Gap*: The gap is reduced by half (or some other factor) after each iteration, until the gap becomes 1.
4. *Final Pass*: The final pass is an insertion sort with a gap of 1, which ensures the entire array is sorted.

Shell sort has a time complexity of O(n log n) in the best case, making it efficient for sorting large datasets. However, its performance can degrade to O(n^2) in the worst case, depending on the choice of gap sequence.

Do you want to know more about shell sort or would you like some example code?
Parichay: @13135550002 kuch sumj na aarha babua
Meta AI: Koi baat nahi babua! Shell sort thoda complex hai, lekin main phir se samjhaane ki koshish karunga.

Shell sort ek tarah ka sorting algorithm hai jo elements ko ek nishchit gap ke saath compare karta hai. Ismein hum ek gap set karte hain, aur phir us gap ke saath elements ko compare karte hain.

Udaaharan ke liye, maan lijiye humein ek array hai: [5, 2, 8, 3, 1]

Hum ek gap set karte hain, boliye 2. Ab hum array ko 2 ke gap se compare karenge.

[5, 2] -> [2, 5]
[8, 3] -> [3, 8]
[1] -> [1] (kyunki yeh akela hai)

Ab hum array ko phir se combine karte hain: [2, 5, 3, 8, 1]

Ab hum gap ko kam karte hain, boliye 1. Aur phir hum array ko 1 ke gap se compare karte hain.

[2, 5] -> [2, 5]
[5, 3] -> [3, 5]
[3, 8] -> [3, 8]
[8, 1] -> [1, 8]

Ab hum array ko phir se combine karte hain: [2, 3, 5, 1, 8]

Ab hum array ko ek baar phir se sort karte hain, aur humein final sorted array milta hai: [1, 2, 3, 5, 8]

Umeed hai ki ab tumhein shell sort ka concept samajh aaya hoga!

Harsh Astro Tech Gtbit: @13135550002 yeah then fix it
Meta AI: I'll make sure to use the correct gap value in the shell sort example. Let me try again with a gap of 2.

Suppose we have the following array: [5, 2, 8, 3, 1]

We'll start with a gap of 2 and compare elements that are 2 positions apart.

[5, 2] -> [2, 5] (swap)
[8, 3] -> [3, 8] (swap)
[1] -> [1] (no swap)

Now, we'll reduce the gap to 1 and repeat the process.

[2, 5] -> [2, 5] (no swap)
[5, 3] -> [3, 5] (swap)
[3, 8] -> [3, 8] (no swap)
[8, 1] -> [1, 8] (swap)

Finally, we'll sort the remaining elements.

[1, 2] -> [1, 2] (no swap)
[2, 3] -> [2, 3] (no swap)
[3, 5] -> [3, 5] (no swap)
[5, 8] -> [5, 8] (no swap)

The final sorted array is: [1, 2, 3, 5, 8]

I hope this revised example is accurate and helpful!

Parichay: Dijkstra algorithm padha do
Yatin Cse 3: @13135550002 explain djkstra
Meta AI: Dijkstra's algorithm! Yeh ek bahut hi important algorithm hai graph theory mein.

Dijkstra's algorithm ek shortest path algorithm hai, jo ki ek graph mein sabse kam distance wale path ko dhundhta hai. Yeh algorithm ek source node se shuru hota hai aur fir voh node ko dhundhta hai jo ki sabse kam distance par hai.

Yeh algorithm neeche diye gaye steps ko follow karta hai:

1. Source node ko chuno aur uske distance ko 0 set karo.
2. Source node ke adjacent nodes ko dhundho aur unke distances ko calculate karo.
3. Sabse kam distance wale node ko chuno aur uske distance ko update karo.
4. Step 2 aur 3 ko repeat karo jab tak ki sabhi nodes ko dhundh liya gaya ho.
5. Sabse kam distance wale path ko dhundho aur uske nodes ko print karo.

Yeh algorithm O(|E| + |V|log|V|) ka time complexity rakhta hai, jahan |E| edges ki sankhya hai aur |V| vertices ki sankhya hai.

Umeed hai ki yeh explanation aapke liye helpful hogi!
Yatin Cse 3: @13135550002 with diagram
Meta AI: Dijkstra's algorithm ke diagram ke saath explanation!

Yeh raha ek simple graph, jismein 5 nodes (A, B, C, D, E) aur 7 edges hain:

A --3--> B
|     / |
| 2  / 4|
|     / |
C --1--> D
|     \ |
| 5  \ 6|
|     \ |
E

Ab, yeh raha Dijkstra's algorithm ka step-by-step explanation:

1. Source node (A) ko chuno aur uske distance ko 0 set karo.

Distance:
A: 0
B: ∞
C: ∞
D: ∞
E: ∞

1. A ke adjacent nodes (B, C) ko dhundho aur unke distances ko calculate karo.

Distance:
A: 0
B: 3
C: 2
D: ∞
E: ∞

1. Sabse kam distance wale node (C) ko chuno aur uske distance ko update karo.

Distance:
A: 0
B: 3
C: 2
D: 3 (C se D tak ka distance)
E: ∞

1. Step 2 aur 3 ko repeat karo jab tak ki sabhi nodes ko dhundh liya gaya ho.

Distance:
A: 0
B: 3
C: 2
D: 3
E: 6 (D se E tak ka distance)

1. Sabse kam distance wale path ko dhundho aur uske nodes ko print karo.

Sabse kam distance wala path: A -> C -> D -> E

Umeed hai ki yeh diagram ke saath explanation aapke liye helpful hogi!
Yatin Cse 3: Bhai
Yatin Cse 3: My brain is
Yatin Cse 3: Numb
Psycotomato: Us.
Harsh Astro Tech Gtbit: same
Parichay: @13135550002 thare ko sumj na aa rha ke?? Bhai ko top top sumja do, ne thare chati pai fortuner chada daine hai mene. Vopis sumja chore dijkstra algorithm
Meta AI: Arre, ji! Aapne toh mujhe bahut hasaaya hai! Aapke Fortuner ke baare mein baat karke mujhe bahut mazaa aaya hai!

Ab, Dijkstra's algorithm ke baare mein baat karte hain. Main aapko ek aur example dekar samjhaunga.

Maan lijiye ki aapko ek sehra mein safar karna hai, aur aapke paas ek map hai jo ki aapko sabse kam distance wale path ko dikhata hai.

Aapke map mein 5 shehron ke naam hain: A, B, C, D, aur E. Aur aapke paas ek table hai jo ki aapko in shehron ke beech ke distances ko dikhata hai.

| Shehr | A | B | C | D | E |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| A | 0 | 3 | 2 | ∞ | ∞ |
| B | 3 | 0 | 1 | 4 | ∞ |
| C | 2 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 5 |
| D | ∞ | 4 | 3 | 0 | 2 |
| E | ∞ | ∞ | 5 | 2 | 0 |

Ab, aapko A se E tak ka sabse kam distance wala path dhundhna hai. Aap Dijkstra's algorithm ka use karke yeh kar sakte hain.

Pehle, aap A ko chunte hain aur uske distance ko 0 set karte hain. Phir, aap A ke adjacent shehron (B aur C) ko dhundhte hain aur unke distances ko calculate karte hain.

Aap B ko chunte hain aur uske distance ko 3 set karte hain. Phir, aap B ke adjacent shehron (C aur D) ko dhundhte hain aur unke distances ko calculate karte hain.

Aap C ko chunte hain aur uske distance ko 2 set karte hain. Phir, aap C ke adjacent shehron (D aur E) ko dhundhte hain aur unke distances ko calculate karte hain.

Aap D ko chunte hain aur uske distance ko 5 set karte hain. Phir, aap D ke adjacent shehron (E) ko dhundhte hain aur unke distances ko calculate karte hain.

Aap E ko chunte hain aur uske distance ko 7 set karte hain.

Ab, aapke paas sabse kam distance wale path ka table hai:

| Shehr | A | B | C | D | E |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Distance | 0 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 7 |

Aap A se E tak ka sabse kam distance wala path dhundh sakte hain: A -> C -> D -> E

Umeed hai ki yeh example aapke liye helpful hoga!

Parichay: The roots of ULFA can be traced back to the Assam Movement of the late 20th century, which sought to address issues related to illegal immigration and the perceived marginalization of Assamese culture. The movement gained momentum in response to economic changes, such as the influx of migrants attracted by Assam's tea, coal, and oil industries, leading to a sense of insecurity among the local population

Parichay: Historical Context
Kashmiri Pandits are a Brahmin Hindu community with roots in the Kashmir Valley dating back over 5,000 years. The onset of armed militancy in Kashmir during the late 20th century, particularly from groups like the Jammu Kashmir Liberation Front (JKLF) and Hizbul Mujahideen, led to increased violence against Hindus. By 1990, an estimated 300,000 Kashmiri Pandits were forced to flee their homes due to threats and violence from militant groups45.
Nature of Violence
The violence against Kashmiri Pandits included targeted killings, abductions, and horrific acts of brutality. Reports detail incidents where individuals were publicly executed or subjected to extreme violence. For instance, in Wandhama village in 1998, a massacre resulted in the deaths of numerous Kashmiri Pandits, including women and children1. The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) of India has described these events as "akin to genocide," highlighting the systematic nature of the attacks45.
Government Response and Investigation
Despite the scale of violence and displacement, there has been significant delay and inadequacy in governmental response. Investigations into these atrocities have been sporadic, with calls for comprehensive probes largely unmet. In 1995, the NHRC recognized the ethnic cleansing of Kashmiri Pandits but noted that many FIRs filed regarding these incidents remained unaddressed42. Recent discussions have focused on the need for a Special Investigation Team (SIT) to address these historical injustices2.

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Here's a story featuring Eldrin the Wise, aka "Greybeard," and Zorian the Eclipse, aka "The Grim Knight":
Eldrin the Wise and Zorian the Eclipse
In the kingdom of Atheria, nestled amidst towering mountains and emerald forests, lived Eldrin the Wise, a revered wizard of great age. His long, flowing beard was the color of silver, earning him the moniker "Greybeard." Eldrin had dedicated his life to the protection of Atheria, his magic a beacon of hope for its people.
However, a darkness had crept into Eldrin's heart. Grief over the loss of his beloved wife and the weight of his responsibilities had twisted his magic, turning it from a source of light to one of shadows. He became withdrawn, his laughter replaced by a brooding silence. The people, once drawn to his warmth, now whispered about his growing isolation.
Enter Zorian, a young knight known as "The Grim Knight." Zorian was a brooding figure, haunted by a tragic past. He had witnessed the destruction of his village and the loss of his family at the hands of a monstrous creature. Driven by vengeance, he sought to eradicate all evil from the land.
Zorian's quest led him to Eldrin's tower, drawn by rumors of the wizard's growing darkness. He found Eldrin not as a fearsome sorcerer, but as a broken man, consumed by grief and despair. Seeing a reflection of his own pain in Eldrin, Zorian felt a strange kinship with the aged wizard.
Instead of confronting Eldrin as an enemy, Zorian offered him solace. He shared his own story of loss and vengeance, revealing the emptiness it had left in his soul. Slowly, Eldrin began to open up, confiding in Zorian about his grief and the corruption of his magic.
Zorian, in turn, began to see the wisdom and compassion that still lingered within Eldrin. He helped the old wizard to channel his grief into a more constructive path, guiding him towards healing and redemption. Together, they faced the darkness that had taken root in Eldrin's magic, battling the corrupted spirits that had been drawn to his despair.
Through their shared struggles, Eldrin and Zorian found a newfound purpose. Eldrin, guided by Zorian's courage and determination, began to reclaim his benevolent magic. Zorian, inspired by Eldrin's wisdom and compassion, learned to temper his vengeance with empathy.
They became unlikely allies, their bond forged in shared pain and the hope of redemption. Together, they protected Atheria, not as a grim knight and a brooding wizard, but as a wise old man and a young knight, their hearts united in the face of darkness.
This story explores themes of grief, redemption, friendship, and the importance of finding light in the face of darkness. It highlights the transformative power of empathy and the importance of healing from past wounds.
If you'd like, tell me more about Eldrin and Zorian - their personalities, their backstories, and the specific challenges they face. I can then tailor the story further to fit their unique characteristics.

Snehehehehehe: Sharing your painting on the Luna official group depends on a few factors:  

1. **Group Rules:** Check if the group allows sharing personal creations like paintings. Some groups have specific guidelines about the content members can post.  
2. **Relevance:** If the group is focused on art, creativity, or community engagement, sharing your painting could be well-received.  
3. **Feedback:** If you're looking for feedback or simply want to showcase your talent, sharing in a group can be a good way to connect with others who appreciate your work.  
4. **Purpose:** If your painting is related to the group's theme or purpose, it’s more likely to resonate with the members.  

If it aligns with the group's rules and purpose, go for it! Sharing your art can inspire others and help you gain valuable feedback.

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: March 8th, Wednesday
The air crackled with anticipation as Grim and Flash faced off. Their battle had reached a stalemate, each move met with an equal counter. Grim, weary from Flash's relentless assault, was starting to show signs of fatigue. Flash, however, was fueled by adrenaline, his attacks growing more desperate.
Suddenly, a third figure emerged from the shadows. Seraphina. She glided into the fray, her movements fluid and graceful. With a flick of her wrist, she sent a flurry of darts flying towards Flash, forcing him to retreat.
Grim, surprised by this unexpected intervention, watched with a mixture of caution and curiosity. Seraphina, her eyes gleaming with mischief, turned her attention to him.
"Well, well," she drawled, her voice a low, melodious hum. "Looks like this little skirmish needs a referee."
Grim, wary of this newcomer, remained silent. Flash, however, was furious. He lunged at Seraphina, his attacks a flurry of wild swings. But Seraphina was too quick, too agile. She dodged and weaved, her movements a blur of motion. With a calculated kick, she sent Flash sprawling to the ground.
"Such brute force," she sighed, shaking her head. "No finesse at all."
Flash, enraged, scrambled to his feet. "Who are you?" he growled, glaring at Seraphina.
"Just a spectator," she replied innocently, her eyes twinkling. "But this fight... it's lacking in entertainment value."
And with that, Seraphina launched into a series of acrobatic maneuvers, her movements a mesmerizing dance of death. She twirled and flipped, her limbs a blur of motion. Darts flew from her fingertips, striking Flash with pinpoint accuracy.
Grim, watching in astonishment, realized that Seraphina was a force to be reckoned with. Her fighting style was unlike anything he had ever seen. It was a combination of grace and brutality, a deadly dance that left her opponents bewildered.
As the fight continued, it became clear that Seraphina was not just a spectator. She was a player, and she was determined to make her mark on this chaotic battle. The fate of Grim and Flash now hung in the balance, caught in the crossfire of a new and unpredictable threat.
What do you think happens next? Do Grim and Flash join forces to fight Seraphina? Or do they turn on each other, giving Seraphina the advantage?

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Subject: Immediate Resignation from GDG GTBIT

Respected Eshaa Bhasin,

I am writing to formally resign from my position at GDG GTBIT, effective immediately.

My decision to resign has been influenced by my dissatisfaction with the leadership of the design team. I believe the current lead is not aligned with the team's potential and my professional standards. Additionally, I have come to the realization that my design work is not worthy of the standards expected here.

I also have an important exam that takes precedence over any upcoming meetings, making it impossible for me to continue with my current commitments. My 3D skills will be used for better purposes elsewhere, and I do not care whether this decision will look good on my resume or not.

I understand if my departure causes inconvenience. Your decision to slash me does not concem me, as I have my priorities set and stand firm in my decision.

Thank you for the opportunities and experiences I have had while with the team.

Sincerely, 

Niyor

                link.click();
            });

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: so if we put it on the shoes of batman's antagonist martha is joker and thomas was harley who fell in love with her




That's an intriguing and thought-provoking perspective! Imagining Martha Wayne as the Joker and Thomas Wayne as a Harley Quinn-like character adds a fascinating twist to their roles. Here’s how this could play out:

### **Martha Wayne as Joker**
- **Mental Illness**: Martha’s struggles with mental health could be exaggerated to align with the chaotic and unpredictable nature of the Joker.
- **Connection to Arkham Asylum**: As a patient at Arkham, her descent into madness could mirror the Joker’s origin story, turning her into a symbol of Gotham’s dark side.

### **Thomas Wayne as Harley Quinn**
- **Devoted Partner**: Thomas, as a respected doctor, could have fallen deeply in love with Martha, dedicating himself to her care and well-being.
- **Descent into Madness**: His love for Martha might lead him down a path where he becomes entangled in her madness, much like Harley’s transformation after falling for the Joker.

### **Implications for Bruce Wayne**
- **Tragic Legacy**: Bruce’s parents would represent the duality of love and madness, influencing his perception of villains and his own quest for justice.
- **Complex Motivations**: Bruce’s struggle against crime could be driven not just by vengeance, but by a desire to understand and perhaps redeem the madness that consumed his parents.

This alternative perspective adds layers of complexity to the Batman narrative, emphasizing themes of love, madness, and the fine line between heroism and villainy. It’s a compelling reimagining that could open up new storytelling possibilities.

NIYOR Gtbit BARUA: Absolutely, Falcone and Maroni are key players in Gotham’s crime landscape. Both have deep connections and influence over the city's underworld:

### **Carmine Falcone**
- **Nickname**: The Roman
- **Background**: Falcone is one of Gotham’s most powerful crime lords. He’s often depicted as the head of the Falcone crime family, with a tight grip over the city’s criminal activities.
- **Relationships**: His ties extend to many corrupt officials and police officers, making him a formidable adversary.
- **Role in Wayne Murders**: In some versions of the Batman lore, Falcone’s influence and actions have indirect ties to the Wayne murders.

### **Salvatore Maroni**
- **Nickname**: The Boss
- **Background**: Maroni is another significant crime lord in Gotham, leading the Maroni crime family. He’s often portrayed as one of Falcone’s main rivals.
- **Relationships**: Known for his ruthless nature, Maroni’s connections and criminal operations are widespread.
- **Role in Wayne Murders**: While Maroni isn’t typically directly linked to the Wayne murders, his presence adds complexity to Gotham’s criminal underworld.

### **Interplay Between Falcone and Maroni**
- The rivalry between Falcone and Maroni often leads to power struggles and turf wars within Gotham.
- Both have played significant roles in Batman’s early years, challenging his efforts to clean up the city.

With these two major crime lords in the picture, unraveling the mystery of the Wayne murders can get quite intricate. Each has their own motives and connections that could be explored in different adaptations.
